A ray of light incident on face AB at an angle θ

For transmission of light through face AC

iAC<iC

or sinA-r1<siniC

By mirror formula

1fmirror =1u+1v

Here u= -a, v=-a3, fmirror= -f2

⇒-2f=1-a+3-a=-4a

⇒-2f=-4a⇒a=2f

subject: Physics
solution:
From the given figure, the interface is horizontal and the normal is along the y-axis.

The incident ray is along the vector $\vec{AO} = 2\hat{i} - 3\hat{j}$.
The angle of incidence $\alpha$ is the angle between the incident ray and the normal.
$\sin \alpha = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{2^2 + (-3)^2}} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{13}}$

The refracted ray is along the vector $\vec{OB} = C\hat{i} - 4\hat{j}$.
The angle of refraction $\beta$ is the angle between the refracted ray and the normal.
$\sin \beta = \dfrac{C}{\sqrt{C^2 + (-4)^2}} = \dfrac{C}{\sqrt{C^2 + 16}}$

Applying Snell's law at the interface:
$\mu_1 \sin \alpha = \mu_2 \sin \beta$
$1 \times \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{13}} = 1.5 \times \dfrac{C}{\sqrt{C^2 + 16}}$
$\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{13}} = \dfrac{3}{2} \dfrac{C}{\sqrt{C^2 + 16}}$

Squaring both sides:
$\dfrac{4}{13} = \dfrac{9}{4} \left( \dfrac{C^2}{C^2 + 16} \right)$
$16(C^2 + 16) = 117 C^2$
$16 C^2 + 256 = 117 C^2$
$101 C^2 = 256$
$C^2 = \dfrac{256}{101}$
$C = \dfrac{16}{\sqrt{101}} \approx 1.592$

Approximating $\sqrt{101} \approx 10$, we get $C \approx 1.6$.

Answer: $1.6$

subject: Physics
options: Focal length of the lens system will change if the positions of two lenses are interchanged
solution:
The equivalent focal length $F$ of two thin lenses in contact is given by $\dfrac{1}{F} = \dfrac{1}{f_1} + \dfrac{1}{f_2}$

For a convex lens, the focal length is positive, so $f_1 = |f_{\text{convex}}|$

For a concave lens, the focal length is negative, so $f_2 = -|f_{\text{concave}}|$

Substituting these values, we get $\dfrac{1}{F} = \dfrac{1}{|f_{\text{convex}}|} - \dfrac{1}{|f_{\text{concave}}|}$

If $|f_{\text{convex}}| > |f_{\text{concave}}|$, then $\dfrac{1}{|f_{\text{convex}}|} < \dfrac{1}{|f_{\text{concave}}|}$

This implies $\dfrac{1}{F} < 0$, so $F$ is negative.

A negative equivalent focal length indicates that the combination behaves as a diverging or concave lens.

Interchanging the positions of the lenses does not change the equivalent focal length since $\dfrac{1}{f_1} + \dfrac{1}{f_2} = \dfrac{1}{f_2} + \dfrac{1}{f_1}$.

Answer: behaves as concave lens if $|f_{\text{convex}}| > |f_{\text{concave}}|$

$\begin{aligned} \therefore & \text { actual } \mathrm{L}_1=84.76-40.51=44.25 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & \text { actual } \mathrm{L}_2=71.61-40.51=31.10 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & \mathrm{~L}_2=\frac{\mathrm{L}_1}{\mu} \\ \Rightarrow & \mu=\frac{\mathrm{L}_1}{\mathrm{~L}_2}=\frac{44.25}{31.10}=1.42\end{aligned}$

$$

$$

Magnification at infinity,

Relative refractive index is μ=μrεr=4×1=2

Now, for complete reflection, θ>θc, where, θc is critical angle.

Now, critical angle is sinθc=1μ=12

 ⇒θc=sin-112⇒θc=30°

So, the angle of incidence for which the total intensity of incident wave will be reflected back into the same medium is θ>30° i.e., 60°.
